skill.name: azure-identity-java
skill.description: Reference guide for implementing Azure Identity authentication patterns in Java applications using the Azure Identity SDK.
skill.summary: Implement Azure Identity authentication in Java with managed identity, service principals, and DefaultAzureCredential.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, network, credential, filesystem, and weak cryptography patterns. Manual review found a Markdown-only Azure Identity reference with sample Java and shell snippets, placeholder secrets, and Azure endpoint examples; no prompt injection, malicious execution, credential exfiltration, or confirmed weak cryptography was found.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Flagged as Command Execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The reported Ruby backtick and PowerShell findings are Markdown formatting, credential names, or documentation that mentions Azure CLI and PowerShell. The skill does not contain executable scripts that run these commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are Markdown code fences, inline code formatting, or plain documentation references. No runnable wrapper script or dynamic command construction is present in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: Credential Environment Variables Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Verdict: NEEDS_REVIEW_WITH_LOW_RISK. The skill lists Azure credential environment variable names and placeholder values for expected SDK configuration. This is legitimate for an Azure Identity guide, but users should avoid committing real secrets or enabling verbose logs in shared environments.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file uses placeholder values such as tenant IDs, client IDs, and secret names rather than real credentials. The context is an authentication reference, so the pattern is expected but still sensitive.

low_findings.title: Certificate and Token Paths Are Placeholder Guidance
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE_WITH_MINOR_SENSITIVITY. Certificate, PFX, and federated token paths are examples for Azure Identity APIs. They do not read files during skill execution, but they refer to sensitive local material that users must protect.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The references are API parameters and environment variable examples, not code that opens files from the host. The residual concern is user education around certificate and token handling.

low_findings.title: Network URLs Are Azure Service Examples
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The hardcoded URLs are placeholders for Azure Storage, Key Vault, localhost redirect flow, Azure Management scope, and Microsoft authority host. No evidence of exfiltration endpoints or hidden outbound calls was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The domains and scopes match normal Azure SDK examples and use placeholders for tenant resources. The file has no code that sends local secrets to a third-party endpoint.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Static analysis flagged weak cryptography, but the reviewed locations describe Azure Identity, interactive browser credentials, or credential selection. No MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or custom cryptographic implementation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines contain descriptive authentication text, not cryptographic algorithms. Manual review found no weak cryptographic primitive or implementation guidance.

content.value_statement: Java teams need reliable Azure authentication without hardcoding credentials. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Azure Identity SDK patterns for local development, managed identity, CI, and AKS.
actual_capabilities: Explains DefaultAzureCredential setup for Java Azure clients.
Compares managed identity, service principal, certificate, CLI, browser, and device code credentials.
Shows environment variable requirements for Azure Identity credential types.
Covers AKS Workload Identity configuration and token file usage.
Includes guidance for sovereign clouds, token caching, logging, and error handling.
Provides a credential selection matrix by deployment environment.
limitations: The skill is a reference guide and does not execute or test Azure authentication flows.
Version guidance is tied to the documented Azure Identity dependency version.
Examples use placeholders and require real Azure tenant, client, and resource configuration.
It does not cover every Azure service client or authorization policy model.

use_cases.title: Add Azure Authentication to a Java Service
use_cases.description: Use DefaultAzureCredential with Azure clients and choose the right production credential path.

use_cases.title: Prepare CI Authentication
use_cases.description: Map required environment variables for service principal authentication in build and release pipelines.

use_cases.title: Migrate to Managed Identity
use_cases.description: Replace stored secrets with managed identity patterns for Azure App Service, Functions, VMs, or AKS.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Credential
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose the correct Azure Identity credential for a Java application running in [environment]. Explain why it fits.

prompt_templates.title: Implement DefaultAzureCredential
prompt_templates.prompt: Show how to configure DefaultAzureCredential for a Java Azure client that connects to [service]. Include local and production notes.

prompt_templates.title: Configure Pipeline Authentication
prompt_templates.prompt: List the Azure Identity environment variables needed for a Java CI pipeline using a service principal. Include security precautions.

prompt_templates.title: Design an AKS Auth Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an Azure Identity authentication approach for a Java service on AKS using Workload Identity. Include fallback and troubleshooting guidance.

output_examples.input: I need Azure authentication for a Java app running on Azure App Service.
output_examples.output: The response recommends DefaultAzureCredential backed by managed identity, explains when to use system or user assigned identity, and lists required Azure role assignments.

output_examples.input: Our Java CI pipeline needs to call Azure APIs with a service principal.
output_examples.output: The response identifies the required tenant, client, and secret environment variables, then adds guidance to store them in the pipeline secret manager.

output_examples.input: We are deploying a Java service to AKS and want to avoid client secrets.
output_examples.output: The response recommends Workload Identity, describes the federated token file concept, and notes how DefaultAzureCredential fits the deployment.

best_practices: Prefer DefaultAzureCredential first, then narrow the chain only when deployment requirements are clear.
Use managed identity or workload identity in production instead of long-lived client secrets.
Keep tenant IDs, client IDs, certificates, and secrets in managed configuration stores.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode client secrets, passwords, certificate passwords, or tenant-specific values in source code.
Do not use username and password authentication for production services.
Do not enable verbose identity logging in shared environments without reviewing exposed account details.

faq.question: What Java package does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers the Azure Identity SDK package com.azure:azure-identity for Java authentication flows.

faq.question: Does this skill run Azure commands?
faq.answer: No. It documents commands and credential classes, but the skill itself is Markdown guidance only.

faq.question: When should I use DefaultAzureCredential?
faq.answer: Use it when one Java application must work across local development and Azure-hosted production environments.

faq.question: Does it include managed identity guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers system assigned and user assigned managed identity patterns for Azure-hosted applications.

faq.question: Can it help with AKS authentication?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Workload Identity guidance and the related Azure federated token file setting.

faq.question: What should users verify before production use?
faq.answer: Verify SDK version, Azure role assignments, tenant configuration, secret storage, and logging settings.
